**I say:** It was also narrated through other chains from Abdullah ibn 'Awf, from his father, from his grandfather, that a man said that to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In it: "It was a great lote-tree on which weapons were hung, so it was named *Dhat Anwat*, and it was worshipped besides Allah."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَجاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ** — "And We delivered the Children of Israel across the sea" [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 138]: From Muhammad ibn Shahrashub: "The Exilarch (رأس الجالوت) said to Ali (عليه السلام): 'You did not remain after your Prophet but thirty years until you struck each other's faces with the sword!' Ali (عليه السلام) replied: 'And your feet had not yet dried from the water of the sea until you said: **اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ** — "Make for us a god like the gods they have."'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "When Moses went out as a delegate to his Lord, he appointed for them thirty days. When Allah added ten to the thirty, his people said: 'Moses has failed us,' so they did what they did."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Bazzar, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu Nu'aym in *al-Hilyah*, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma' wa al-Sifat* narrated from Jabir who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When Allah spoke to Moses on the day of Tur, He spoke to him with a speech different from the speech with which He spoke to him on the day He called him. Moses said to Him: "O Lord, is this Your speech with which You spoke to me?" He said: "O Moses, I only spoke to you with the power of ten thousand tongues, and I possess the power of all tongues and more powerful than that."'"

"When Moses returned to the Children of Israel, they said: 'O Moses, describe for us the speech of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن).' He said: 'You cannot endure it. Have you not seen the sounds of thunderbolts—that which comes with the sweetest sweetness you have heard? That is close to it, but it is not it.'"

**I say:** As for the end of the narration, it is a metaphor for approximation and there is no harm in it. As for its beginning, there is some obscurity; perhaps what is meant by the power of ten thousand tongues is the power of conveying understanding (التفهيم) found in ten thousand if they supported one another. For the tongues of people differ in their power of conveying understanding. Thus, the meaning is that it equals, in terms of providing understanding and revealing the intent, ten thousand tongues if they were gathered together.

Accordingly, the meaning of the difference in his saying: "He spoke to him with a speech different from the speech with which He spoke to him on the day He called him" is the variation in the manner of conveying understanding.

In *al-Ma'ani*: With its chain to Hisham who said: "I was with al-Sadiq Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام) when Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb and Abd al-Malik ibn A'yan entered. Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, what do you say regarding the reported news that the Messenger (ص) saw his Lord? In what form did he see Him? And regarding the news reported that the believers will see their Lord in Paradise? In what form will they see Him?' He smiled and then said: 'O Mu'awiyah, how unseemly it is for a man to reach seventy or eighty years of age, living in the kingdom of Allah and eating from His blessings, and then not know Allah with His true recognition (حق معرفته).' Then he said: 'O Mu'awiyah, Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not see the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, with ocular witnessing (مشاهدة العيان). Vision is of two kinds: vision of the heart (رؤية القلب) and vision of the eye (رؤية البصر). Whoever means vision of the heart is correct, and whoever means vision of the eye has lied and disbelieved in Allah and His signs, because of the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Whoever likens Allah to His creation has disbelieved."'"

"My father narrated to me from his father, from al-Husayn ibn Ali (عليه السلام) who said: 'Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) was asked: "O brother of the Messenger of Allah, have you seen your Lord?" He replied: "I would not worship a Lord I have not seen. The eyes do not see Him with ocular witnessing, but the hearts see Him through the realities of faith (حقائق الإيمان)."'"

"If the believer were to see his Lord with ocular vision, then everyone upon whom vision and sight are possible is a creature, and a creature must have a creator. You would then have made Him a temporal phenomenon (محدثا) and a creature. And whoever likens Him to His creation has taken a partner with Allah."

"Woe to them! Have they not heard the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ** — 'The sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware' [Qarai: Surah al-An'am, 103], and His saying to Moses: **لَنْ تَرانِي وَلكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ ـ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرانِي ـ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّى رَبُّهُ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسى صَعِقاً** — 'You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me. So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it, and Moses fell down swooning' [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 143]. He only manifested of His light upon the mountain as much as the light that passes through the eye of a needle, yet the earth was crushed, the mountains collapsed, and « *Moses fell down swooning* »—meaning dead. « *When he recovered* » and his soul was returned to him, « *he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence’* » from the words of those who claim You can be seen, and I return to my recognition of You: that sights do not perceive You, « *and I am the first of the believers* » that You see and are not seen, and You are in the highest prospect (الحديث)."

In *al-Tawhid*: With its chain to Ali (عليه السلام) in a narration: "And Moses asked, and there flowed upon his tongue from the praise of Allah (عز وجل): « *My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You* ». That request of his was a momentous matter, and he asked for a grave thing, so he was rebuked. Allah (عز وجل) said: « *You shall not see Me* » in this world until you die and see Me in the Hereafter. 'But if you wish to see Me, then « *look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me* ».' Then Allah displayed some of His signs and our Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, and the mountain was shattered and became dust, « *and Moses fell down swooning* ». Then Allah revived him and raised him, and he said: « *Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers* »—meaning the first of them to believe in You that He is not seen."

**I say:** The two narrations—as you see—support what was mentioned in the previous commentary. From them, several points are concluded:

First: That the request was only for the vision of the heart, not the vision of the eye, which is impossible for Him, the Exalted, in whatever way it is conceived. Far be it from the station of the one who spoke with Allah (الكليم) (عليه السلام) to be ignorant of that which is self-evident regarding the sanctified presence of his Lord, while he calls the people he chose for the appointed time "fools" (سفهاء) when they asked for vision, saying to his Lord: **أَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا** — "Will You destroy us because of what the fools amongst us have done?" [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 155]. How then could he himself proceed with what he called foolishness?

The dispute and controversy in the first century, especially from the time of the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them) to the time of al-Rida (peace be upon him), regarding this issue reached its peak. The Mu'tazilites denied it absolutely, while the Ash'arites affirmed it in the Hereafter. There was another group that affirmed it in both this world and the Hereafter. Both sides sought evidence from the verse. The dispute remained standing and did not cease outwardly except by the swords of the Ayyubids, who annihilated the Mu'tazilites and brought their star to its setting.

The gist of the Mu'tazilites' argument was that they relied on His saying in the verse: « *You shall not see Me* » and other texts that negate ocular vision through reason and tradition, interpreting the verses and narrations that suggest its permissibility. The gist of the Ash'arites' argument was that they relied on the comparison made in the verse: « *But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me* » and other verses and some narrations regarding its permissibility in the Hereafter, interpreting everything else according to their dialectical methods. Sometimes it was argued that there is no evidence that ocular vision must be restricted to corporeal things, so it is possible for it to relate to non-material matters. And that sight relates to substance (الجوهر) and accident (العرض), and there is no commonality between them except absolute existence (الموجود المطلق); thus, every existent can be the object of sight even if it is not a body or corporeal.

The falsity of these two arguments and similar ones has become so clear in these times that it almost reaches the level of self-evidence.

In any case, we are not concerned with presenting the refutations and affirmations they brought from both sides; whoever wishes to examine them can refer to the books of theology (الكلام) and the lengthy commentaries of both schools.

What has been concluded from our previous inquiry is: First, that ocular vision—whether it remains as it is today or transforms into any other state where it is material and natural, relating to measure, shape, color, and light, operated by a material natural organ—is impossible to relate to Allah, the Glorified, in this world or the Hereafter. This is proven by rational proof (البرهان) and what has been narrated of verses and traditions negating vision. Yes, there is a specific necessary knowledge (علم ضروري خاص) relating to Him, the Exalted, other than the necessary knowledge attained through deduction, which is called "vision" (رؤية). This is what is meant by the verses and narrations that appear to affirm vision, due to the many explicit context-clues (القرائن) within them, and the locus of this recognition is the Hereafter.

Second: That His saying, the Exalted: « *My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You* » is entirely foreign to sensory ocular vision, whether in affirmation, negation, request, or response. Rather, the discourse revolves around vision in the other sense, which is the vision of the heart (رؤية القلب) according to the terminology used in the narrations.

Al-Saduq narrated in *al-Uyun*: Regarding what al-Ma'mun asked al-Rida (عليه السلام)—that he answered the question of vision in the verse by saying that Moses only asked that on behalf of his people, not for himself. For when they said, "Show us Allah openly," and the thunderbolt took them, then Allah revived them, they asked Moses to ask for himself. He informed them of its impossibility, but they insisted, so he said: « *My Lord, show Yourself* »—meaning, according to what my people propose.

The narration, as we pointed out in the reports of Adam's Paradise, is weak in its chain (ضعيفة السند). Furthermore, it does not agree with the established principles in the reports of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). Their reports, especially the sermons of Ali and al-Rida (عليه السلام), are filled with the mention of self-disclosure (التجلي) and heart-vision. Thus, there is no reason for him (عليه السلام) to commit to the vision mentioned in the verse being ocular vision in both request and response, and then answering through a dialectical method that does not well fit the verse, as it is completely contrary to its literal meaning (ظاهرها) and contrary to the apparent state of Moses. For if they had proposed that to him, he would have rebuked them as he rebuked them by saying: **إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ** — "Indeed you are an ignorant people" when they said: **يا مُوسَى اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ** — "O Moses, make for us a god like the gods they have."

Third: It is concluded from the two narrations that Moses (عليه السلام) was not granted vision in the aforementioned sense in this world, but was granted it in the Hereafter. It appears this is derived from His saying, the Exalted: « *So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it, and Moses fell down swooning* ». For the condition in His saying: « *But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me* » implies that what was assumed for the mountain is exactly like what was assumed for Moses; it cannot endure the manifestation and the will just as he cannot. The self-disclosure occurred to the mountain, and it was leveled and he swooned. Had it occurred to Moses, he too would have been leveled and swooned. Thus, self-disclosure (التجلي) is possible in itself, but in relation to the one to whom it is disclosed, it necessitates leveling and swooning. This suggests that self-disclosure is not prevented in itself along with swooning and death. Narrations have reached the level of being widely transmitted (استفاضت) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, discloses Himself to the people of Paradise, and that they have a visitation every Friday, as occurred in His saying, the Exalted: **وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ** — "Some faces on that day will be fresh, looking towards their Lord" [Qarai: Surah al-Qiyamah, 22-23].

Fourth: It is concluded from the two narrations that the swooning (صعقة) of Moses (عليه السلام) was death, after which Allah returned his soul to him, not a mere fainting spell.

Fifth: That what he (عليه السلام) mentioned—that He disclosed of His light an amount equal to what passes through the eye of a needle—is of the nature of representing a meaning through sensory matters. His light is not a sensory light, nor is it measured by a sensory thing like the eye of a needle. Therefore, this was represented in another narration by placing the tip of the thumb on the fingertip of the pinky, as will come. The purpose in any case is to explain its smallness and insignificance [relative to His totality].

In any case, the self-disclosure is only what suffices for his leveling and swooning. As for the perfection of His light, the Exalted, it is infinite (غير متناه) and no assumed finite matter can correspond to it; there is no ratio between the finite and the infinite.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn 'Adi in *al-Kamil*, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim (who graded it authentic), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Kitab al-Ru'yah* narrated through various chains from Anas ibn Malik: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read this verse: « *So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it* ». He said: 'Like this,' and signaled with his two fingers, placing the tip of his thumb on the fingertip of the pinky—and in one wording: on the highest joint of the pinky—and the mountain sank and Moses fell down swooning. In another wording: the mountain sank into the earth, and it continues to descend into it until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** It occurs in the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that the mountain was leveled and became dust. In some, it sank into the sea and continues to descend until this hour. In some: "until this hour." What is concluded from interpreting them by one another is that it became dust and descended into the sea, so no trace of it is ever seen. This should be the meaning of the saying: "The mountain sank into the earth or the sea, and it continues to sink until the Day of Resurrection or until the hour."

In it: Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through Thabit from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: « *So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain* ». He said: "He manifested an amount like this," and he placed the thumb on the pinky finger. Humayd—the narrator of the hadith—said: "O Abu Muhammad—the narrator from Anas—what do you intend by this?" He struck his chest and said: "Who are you, O Humayd? And what are you, O Humayd? Anas ibn Malik narrates to me from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and you say: 'What do you intend by this?'"

In it: al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in *Nawadir al-Usul* and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Hilyah* narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this verse: « *My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You* ». He said: 'Allah (عز وجل) said: "O Moses, no living thing sees Me but that it dies, no dry thing but that it crumbles, and no moist thing but that it scatters. Only the people of Paradise see Me—those whose eyes do not die and whose bodies do not decay."'"

**I say:** The narration is similar to what was previously mentioned from the narration of *al-Tawhid* from Ali (عليه السلام), and the explanation of its meaning has been provided.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "When Moses asked his Lord, Blessed and Exalted: « *He said, ‘My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You.’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me.’* » He said: 'When Moses ascended the mountain, the gates of heaven were opened, and the angels approached in throngs with pillars in their hands, at the tops of which was light. They passed by him throng after throng, saying: "O son of Imran, stand firm, for you have asked for a momentous thing." He said: 'Moses remained standing until our Lord, Majestic is His Majesty, disclosed Himself. He leveled the mountain and Moses fell down swooning. When Allah returned his soul to him and he recovered, « *he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers’* ».'"

In it also: From Abu Basir who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) saying: 'When Moses ibn Imran asked his Lord to look at Him, Allah promised that He would sit in a place. Then He commanded the angels to pass by him procession after procession—with thunder, lightning, wind, and thunderbolts. Every time a procession passed by him, his limbs trembled. He would raise his head and ask: "Which of you is my Lord?" It would be answered: "He is coming, and you have asked for a momentous thing, O son of Imran."'"

**I say:** The narration is fabricated (موضوعة), and what it contains cannot be reconciled with any of the established principles derived from the Book and the Sunnah.

In *al-Basa'ir*: With its chain to Abu Muhammad Abdullah ibn Abi Abdullah al-Farisi and others, who raised it to Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): "The Cherubim (الكروبيون) are a group of our Shi'a from the first creation. Allah placed them behind the Throne. If the light of one of them were divided among the people of the earth, it would suffice them." Then he said: "When Moses (عليه السلام) asked his Lord what he asked, He commanded one of the Cherubim to disclose himself to the mountain, and he leveled it."

**I say:** The gist of the narration is that His self-disclosure, the Glorified, accepts intermediaries (الوسائط), just as other matters attributed to Him, the Exalted—such as taking souls, giving life, providing sustenance, and revelation—accept intermediaries. Thus, He, the Exalted, discloses Himself through intermediaries just as He takes souls through the Angel of Death, gives life through the Caller of the Trumpet, provides sustenance through Michael, and reveals through Gabriel, the Trustworthy Spirit. The explanation of this narration will come in a suitable place, God willing. The Cherubim are mentioned in the Torah.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Mardawayh and al-Hakim (who graded it authentic) narrated from Anas: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read « *dakkan* » (دكّاً) with nunation and did not elongate it."

In it: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read: « *So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it* »—reading it as *dakkan* (دكّاً) with doubling and elongation.

In it: Abu Nu'aym in *al-Hilyah* narrated from Mu'awiyah ibn Qurrah from his father who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, six mountains flew away due to His greatness and fell in Medina: Uhud, Warqan, and Radwa. And there fell in Mecca: Thawr, Thabir, and Hira.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In it: al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When Allah disclosed Himself to Moses, seven mountains flew apart. Five of them are in the Hijaz and two in Yemen. In the Hijaz: Uhud, Thabir, Hira, Thawr, and Warqan. In Yemen: Husur and Sayr."

**I say:** Other things have been narrated regarding the shattering of the mountain. These narrations, despite their differences in the number of mountains that flew apart, if intended as an explanation for the leveling of the mountain, do not align with the verse. If something else is intended, while it is possible to occur, such isolated reports (الآحاد) are not sufficient to prove it.

Similarly, what has been narrated through Shi'a and Sunni paths that the tablets of the Torah were of emerald; and in some Sunni paths from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The tablets revealed to Moses were from the lote-tree of Paradise; the length of a tablet was twelve cubits." In some: "Allah wrote the tablets for Moses while he heard the scratching of the pens on the tablets." In some of our reports: "These tablets are buried in a mountain in Yemen," or "A rock there swallowed them and they are preserved in its belly," and other such isolated reports not supported by definitive context-clues. Furthermore, exegetical inquiry does not depend on delving into them.

In *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: It is said: "From Ali (كرم الله وجهه): that he read *ju'ar* (جؤار) with a *dammah* on the *jim* and a *hamzah*. He said: 'It is a loud sound.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: « *And he threw down the tablets* » the verse: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bazzar, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'May Allah have mercy on Moses; the one who witnesses is not like the one who is informed. His Lord, Blessed and Exalted, informed him that his people were tried after him, yet he did not throw down the tablets. But when he saw them and witnessed them, he threw down the tablets and some of them were broken.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*: From Muhammad ibn Abi Hamzah from someone who mentioned it from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "When Allah, Blessed and Exalted, informed Moses that his people had taken up a calf [a body] that had a lowing sound—it did not affect him like the position of witnessing. But when he saw them, his anger intensified and he threw the tablets from his hand." Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: "Witnessing has a merit over report."

In *al-Kafi*: With its chain to Sufyan ibn 'Uyaynah from al-Suddi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "No servant purifies his faith in Allah for forty days—or he said: No servant makes the remembrance of Allah sincere for forty days—but that Allah makes him detached from the world, shows him its disease and its cure, establishes wisdom in his heart, and makes his tongue speak with it."

Then he recited: « *Indeed those who took up the calf shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath and abasement in the life of this world. Thus do We requite the fabricators.* » "So you will not see a person of innovation (صاحب بدعة) except that he is abased, nor a fabricator against Allah (عز وجل), His Messenger, and the People of his House except that he is abased."

## (Another Narrative Discourse)

In which we present some of what has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) regarding the meaning of the vision of the heart.

In *al-Tawhid* and *al-Amali*: With its chain to al-Rida (عليه السلام) in a sermon of his, he said: "One (أحد), not by the interpretation of number; Manifest (ظاهر), not by the interpretation of direct contact; Disclosed (متجل), not by the beginning of vision; Hidden (باطن), not by separation."

**I say:** The discourse on His constant self-disclosure to His creation is repeated in the words of Ali and the Imams from his progeny (عليه السلام). We have quoted fragments of his words (عليه السلام) in the discussions on monotheism (التوحيد) under His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ** — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" [Qarai: Surah al-Ma'idah, 73].

In *al-Tawhid*: With its chain to al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his on monotheism: "One, Besought (صمد), Eternal, Everlasting; He has no shadow to hold Him, yet He holds things by their shadows (أظلتها). He is Knower of the unknown, Known to every ignorant one; He is not in His creation, nor is His creation in Him."

**I say:** His saying (عليه السلام) "Known to every ignorant one" is apparent in that He, the Exalted, has a recognition (معرفة) with His creation that is never overtaken by heedlessness, nor shrouded by ignorance. If it were the recognition attained through deduction, it would vanish with the vanishing of its form from the mind. This is if the meaning of his saying "Known to every ignorant one" is that man is ignorant of everything but is not ignorant of his Lord. But if the meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, is known to everyone who is ignorant of Him, then the fact that this recognition is other than the recognition attained through deduction is even more apparent.

His saying (عليه السلام): "He has no shadow to hold Him, yet He holds things by their shadows"—"Shadows" (الأظلة) and "Shadow" (الظلال) are terms used by them (عليه السلام). What is meant by the shadow of a thing is its limit (حده). Therefore, it is negated for Allah, the Glorified, and established for others. Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (عليه السلام) explained it in some (1) narrations of the particles (الذر) and the clay (الطينة), where he mentioned: "Allah created a group of His creation from the clay of Paradise, and another group from the clay of Fire—then He sent them into the shadows (الظلال)." It was asked: "And what are the shadows?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "Have you not seen your shadow in the sun—a thing and yet not a thing?" Thus, existential limits (الحدود الوجودية), in view of the existence of things, are other than Him and yet not other than Him. By them, things are determined, and without them, they would be naught. Perhaps the terminology is taken from the verse of shadows.

In *al-Irshad* and others: From Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his: "Indeed Allah is too majestic to be veiled from anything, or for anything to be veiled from Him."

From him (عليه السلام): "I have not seen a thing except that I saw Allah before it."

From him: "I would not worship a Lord I have not seen."

In *al-Nahj*: From him: "The eyes do not see Him with ocular witnessing, but the hearts have seen Him through the realities of faith."

In *al-Tawhid*: With its chain to Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said: "I asked him about Allah (عز وجل), whether the believers will see Him on the Day of Resurrection? He said: 'Yes, and they have seen Him before the Day of Resurrection.' I said: 'When?' He said: 'When He said to them: **أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ**؟**قالُوا بَلى** — "Am I not your Lord?" They said, "Yes."' Then he was silent for a moment and said: 'And indeed the believers see Him in this world before the Day of Resurrection. Do you not see Him at this very moment?'"

"I said: 'May I narrate this from you?' He said: 'No, for if you narrate it, an ignorant denier of the meaning of what you say will deny it, and then assume that it is anthropomorphism (تشبيه) and disbelief. Vision of the heart is not like vision of the eye—Exalted is Allah above what the anthropomorphists and heretics describe.'"

**I say:** It is clear from the narration that this vision is not the belief and heart-faith acquired through evidence, just as it is not sensory ocular vision. The reason for not frequently using the word "vision" (رؤية) in His case, the Exalted, and not publicizing this usage, is the immediate shift of the word in common understanding to sensory vision, which is negated from His sanctified presence. Otherwise, the reality of vision is established, which is the attaining of a thing through cognitive witnessing (المشاهدة العلمية) not through the path of intellectual deduction. Rather, there are several reports that deny that Allah, the Glorified, is known and recognized through the path of thought, and some of them will follow.

In *al-Tawhid*: With its chain to Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his on monotheism: "There is no veil between Him and His creation except His creation. He is veiled without a veiled veil, and hidden without a hidden screen. There is no god but He, the Great, the All-exalted."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated from al-Rida (عليه السلام) as found in *al-Ilal* and *Jawami' al-Tawhid*.

The noble narration explains the meaning of attaining recognition of Him, the Exalted—a recognition that does not accept ignorance, nor is it overtaken by cessation, change, or error at all. It clarifies that Allah, the Glorified, is not veiled from anything except by that thing itself. Thus, turning toward things is the obstacle to turning toward witnessing Him, the Exalted. Then he (عليه السلام) judged that this screening veil is not a barrier in reality; it is a veil that does not veil and a screen that does not screen.

The result of the two discourses is that He, the Glorified, is witnessed by His creation, known to them, not absent from them. However, their preoccupation with themselves and their turning toward their own essences veiled them from noticing that they witness Him always. Thus, knowledge (العلم) is ever-present, while the knowledge of the knowledge (العلم بالعلم) is missing at times. al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) built upon this foundation in his answer to someone who complained to him of the abundance of doubts. He (عليه السلام) said to him: "Have you ever boarded a ship that broke and sank, and you remained alone on a wooden plank from it while the waves played with you—and you were cut off from every means of salvation?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Did your heart at that moment attach itself to something?" He said: "Yes." He said: "That thing is Allah" (1).

In *Jawami' al-Tawhid*: From al-Rida (عليه السلام) who said: "Allah's creation of the creation is a veil between Him and them."

In *al-Ilal*: With its chain to al-Thumali who said: "I said to Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام): 'For what reason did Allah (عز وجل) veil the creation from Himself?' He said: 'Because Allah, Blessed and Exalted, built them upon a foundation of ignorance (الجهل).'"

**I say:** It appears from the previous narration of *al-Tawhid* that building them upon ignorance is creating them such that they are preoccupied with themselves.

In *al-Mahasin*: With its chain to Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "Indeed Allah (عز وجل) was, and there was nothing other than Him—a light in which there is no darkness, truthful in whom there is no lie, knowing in whom there is no ignorance, and living in whom there is no death. And so He is today, and so He shall remain forever (الحديث)."

In *al-Tawhid*: With its chain to al-Rida (عليه السلام) in a narration: "When the Messenger (ص) looked at his Lord with his heart, He placed him in a light like the light of the veils until what was in the veils became clear to him."

In it also: With its chain to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl who said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام): 'Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) see his Lord (عز وجل)?' He said: 'Yes, he saw Him with his heart. Have you not heard Allah (عز وجل) saying: **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى** — "The heart did not lie about what it saw" [Qarai: Surah an-Najm, 11]? He did not see Him with the sight, but he saw Him with the heart (الفؤاد).'"

In it: With its chain to Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a narration: "Whoever claims that he knows Allah through a veil (حجاب), a form (صورة), or a likeness (مثال) is a polytheist (مشرك), because the veil, the likeness, and the form are other than Him. He is only One, Unified. How then can he unify Him who claims he recognized Him through other than Him? Only he has recognized Allah who recognized Him by Allah (عرفه بالله). So whoever does not recognize Him by Him does not recognize Him; he only recognizes something else. There is nothing between the Creator and the creature. Allah is the Creator of things, not from a thing."

"He is named by His names, so He is other than His names, and the names are other than Him. The described (الموصوف) is other than the describer (الواصف). So whoever claims that he believes in what he does not know is straying from recognition. no creature perceives anything except by Allah, and the recognition of Allah is not perceived except by Allah. Allah is distinct from His creation, and His creation is distinct from Him."

**I say:** The narration establishes the recognition of Allah for every creature that perceives anything of things. It establishes that this recognition is other than the intellectual recognition (المعرفة الفكرية) that is attained through the path of proofs and signs, and that restricting oneself to deductive recognition is not free from ignorance of Allah and hidden polytheism (شرك خفي).

The explanation of this, according to the premises provided by the narration, is that the recognition relating to a thing is the perception of it. Thus, what falls within the locus of perception is that to which recognition truly relates, not anything else. If we assume that we recognized a thing through another thing that is an intermediary in its recognition, then what our perception related to is the intermediary, not the object that possesses the intermediary. If recognition through the intermediary were nonetheless recognition of the object, it would necessitate that the intermediary be, in some way, the object itself, so that recognition of one is exactly the recognition of the other. Thus, it is the object in one respect and not in another, being a linking intermediary between the two things. The external Zayd whom we conceive in our minds is Zayd himself; if it were other than him, we would not have conceived him but conceived something else, and our knowledge would then turn into ignorance.

Since there is no intermediary between the Creator and the creature to serve as a link between them, it is impossible to recognize Him, the Glorified, through something other than Himself. If He were recognized through something, that thing would be Himself. If He is not recognized by Himself, He will never be recognized through anything else. Thus, the claim that He, the Exalted, is known through something—such as a conception (تصور), an affirmation (تصديق), or an external sign—is hidden polytheism, because it is the establishment of an intermediary between the Creator and the creature that is other than both of them. That which is so described does not depend for its existence on the Creator, the Exalted; thus, it is like Him and a partner to Him. Therefore, if Allah, the Glorified, is recognized, He is recognized by His essence (بذاته); and if He is not recognized by His essence, He is not recognized by anything else at all. But He, the Glorified, is known; thus, He is known by His essence—meaning that His transcendent essence and His being known are one and the same thing. Therefore, it is impossible for Him to be unknown, because the establishment of His essence is the very establishment of His being known.

As for the explanation of His being known, it is because no created thing is independent of Him, the Exalted, in its essence in any way, neither externally nor mentally. Its existence is like a relation (النسبة) and a link (الربط) that cannot be independent of its term in any way. Thus, when the knowledge of a creature relates to something—meaning the object of knowledge falls within the locus of its knowledge—it is not realized there except that with it is a Creator upon Whom its existence relies; otherwise, it would be independent without Him. Thus, a knower does not find his object of knowledge except that he has found Allah, the Glorified, before it. The knower himself, being a creature, does not possess independence in knowledge except by Allah, the Glorified, Who sustains the existence of this knower. If he were independent in it without Him, he would be independent without Him and not created by Him. Thus, the knower needs Allah in being a knower, just as the existence of the object of knowledge depends on Him in being an object of knowledge. That is, knowledge relates to the independence of the essence of the object of knowledge; meaning that Allah, the Glorified, is the object of knowledge first, and the object of knowledge is known by Him second. Just as He, the Exalted, is the Knower first, and by Him the thing becomes a knower second. Understand this and contemplate His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِما شاءَ** — "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" [Qarai: Surah al-Baqarah, 255], and in his saying (عليه السلام): "I have not seen a thing except that I saw Allah before it."

It has thus become clear that He, the Exalted, is known, because the establishment of any knowledge of any object of knowledge in reality is not completed except by His being known first, and the establishment of that is necessary.

His saying (عليه السلام): "Whoever claims that he knows Allah through a veil, a form, or a likeness is a polytheist"—what is meant by the veil (الحجاب) is the thing assumed as a separator between Him, the Exalted, and the knower. By the form (الصورة) is meant the mental form accompanied by sensory attributes of lights, colors, and measures. By the likeness (المثال) is meant what is of intellectual meanings that are not sensory. Or perhaps by form is meant the sensory form, and by likeness the imagined form. Or by form is meant conception (التصور) and by likeness affirmation (التصديق). In any case, intellectual sciences (العلوم الفكرية) are included in this. The reports negating that intellectual knowledge constitutes a cognitive encompassment (إحاطة علمية) of Allah are very many.

The reason this recognition is polytheism is that it establishes something that is neither creator nor creature, as you have just learned, and necessitates its being a participant with Him in one respect and distinct from Him in another. Therefore, he (عليه السلام) followed the discourse by saying: "He is only One, Unified"—meaning that nothing shares in His essence in any way that would necessitate His being composite and negate His unity, just as the cognitive form shares with the external object of knowledge in its meaning and quiddity (ماهيته) and differs from it in its existence, whereby the object of knowledge becomes composite of quiddity and existence.

"How then can he unify Him who claims he recognized Him through other than Him?" while establishing a partner for Him in His existence and a composition for Him in His essence. "Only he has recognized Allah who recognized Him by Allah"—meaning by His very essence without an intermediary. "And whoever does not recognize Him by Him does not recognize Him; he only recognizes something else." All of this is "because there is nothing between the Creator and the creature"—meaning any matter that links them which is other than both of them. "And Allah is the Creator of things, not from a thing" that would be a link between them, bringing the Creator to the creature and vice versa, just as the human artisan is linked to his product by the mental image in the artisan's mind and the external material in his hand.

His saying (عليه السلام): "He is named by His names, so He is other than His names" is in the position of repelling a presumed objection, which is to say: "We only recognize Him, the Glorified, by His names that narrate His beauty and majesty." He repelled it by stating that the very act of being named by names dictates that the names are other than Him; for if they were not other than Him, recognizing Him by His names would be recognizing Him by Himself, not by something else. Then he emphasized it by stating that names are describing (واصفة) and the essence is described (موصوفة), "and the described is other than the describer."

If the objector returns and says: "We believe in that which we are ignorant of, and which we cannot know in its essence except through what is termed 'knowledge' by way of metaphor (المجاز)—such as knowledge through the signs (الآيات)"—then he has claimed to believe in what he does not know. Such a person is astray from true knowledge (المعرفة), unaware of what he says; for he inevitably perceives something and has no way to deny that. "And no creature perceives anything except through Allah," so he knows Allah, otherwise he would not be able to know anything through Him. "And the knowledge of Allah is neither attained nor perceived except through Allah," and there is no common link (رابطة مشتركة) between the Creator and the creature, for "Allah is distinct (خلو) from His creation, and His creation is distinct from Him."

It is thus established from the narration that the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified, is necessary (ضروري) for every sentient perceiver among His creation, though many of them are astray from this knowledge and confused regarding it. The one who truly knows Allah (العارف بالله) knows Him through Him, and realizes that he knows Him and knows everything else through Him. There are other narrations that carry some of these meanings.

Know that the narrations transmitted through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are very numerous, and there is no need to cite them all despite their abundance.

Know also that we have not provided a separate philosophical discussion on the issue of vision (الرؤية), because the explanation (البيان) contained in most of the narrations we have cited is itself a philosophical explanation; thus, there was no pressing need to conduct a separate inquiry.
